name: Chandrahas
description: Chandrahas, meaning 'laughter of the moon' or 'moon-sword', is a legendary sword of immense power in Hindu mythology. It is most famously associated with Ravana, the ten-headed demon king of Lanka. The sword was originally given to Ravana by Lord Shiva himself, as a boon for Ravana's intense devotion and penance. Chandrahas is not merely a weapon of destruction; it embodies the power of Shiva and the boon granted to Ravana, representing both divine grace and immense destructive potential. The sword is described as being incredibly sharp and capable of cleaving through any obstacle. Its power is not just physical; it also grants the wielder a certain level of invincibility and enhanced combat prowess. The legend of Chandrahas is intertwined with Ravana's hubris and eventual downfall, serving as a cautionary tale about the misuse of power and the consequences of arrogance. Despite its divine origins, the sword ultimately became a symbol of Ravana's ego and his defiance of dharma. The loss of Chandrahas is often seen as a turning point in the Ramayana, signaling the decline of Ravana's power and the eventual victory of Rama. Its crescent shape is reminiscent of the moon, and its association with Shiva connects it to cosmic power and destruction. The sword's legend continues to resonate, reminding us of the complex interplay between devotion, power, and the ultimate triumph of good over evil.
subtitle: The Moon-Sword of Ravana
material: Divine Metal (believed to be celestial iron or a similar material)
origin: Gifted to Ravana by Lord Shiva as a boon for his devotion.
currentLocation: Unknown. Lost after Ravana's defeat in the Ramayana.

keyMyths.title: Ravana's Penance
keyMyths.description: Ravana performed intense penance to Lord Shiva, sacrificing his own heads as offerings. Pleased with his devotion, Shiva granted him Chandrahas as a boon, a weapon of immense power.

keyMyths.title: Hanuman's Encounter
keyMyths.description: In some versions, Hanuman encounters Chandrahas during his search for Sita in Lanka. The sword's power is evident, but Hanuman, being a devotee of Rama, is unaffected by its aura.

sources.author: Valmiki
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Ravana and his possession of Chandrahas.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of Ravana and his possession of Chandrahas.

sources.author: Tulsidas
sources.description: A popular retelling of the Ramayana, providing further details about Ravana's power and the significance of Chandrahas.
